findings.rationale: Both properties represent numeric ratings, but the OUR term is a specific sub‑rating for non‑textile parts whereas ratingValue is a generic content rating.
findings.qaRationale: OUR term is a narrow ISO 15487 sub-rating constrained to a specific class (eutex:VisualInspectionResult) for non-textile parts, while schema:ratingValue is a generic content-rating value with no domain alignment, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Garment Type covers the overall category of a garment, whereas Clothing Cut specifies only the silhouette or cut detail, making it a narrower special case.
findings.qaRationale: Garment type (jacket/trousers/shirt) names the kind of garment, while clothingCut describes the silhouette/fit (boot cut, loose fit) within a garment — orthogonal facets, not a broader/narrower or overlapping concept.

findings.rationale: Garment Type is a specific garment category, while schema.org’s category is a general classification that can encompass many item types.
findings.qaRationale: garmentType is a specialized garment-classification slot that is subsumed by schema.org's general-purpose category property, so ours is narrower than the target.

findings.rationale: A dyeing facility is a specific type of manufacturing plant, making it more specialized than the general Has Manufacturing Plant property.
findings.qaRationale: Both are product-to-facility properties and dyeing/printing is a manufacturing step, so eutex:dyeingFacility is a role-specialized subproperty of the general gs1:manufacturingPlant relation.

findings.rationale: Dyeing Facility is a specific type of facility where production occurs, whereas producedAtFacility is a general property for any production location.
findings.qaRationale: Our term's range is oec:FacilityInformation (a facility record) whereas untp:producedAtFacility links to a production facility entity, and dyeing/printing is a specific downstream finishing step not equivalent to generic production, so they are not the same property concept.

findings.rationale: Both describe the recycling process applicable to a material, but our term focuses on product fiber composition while GS1’s property is specific to packaging recycling process types.
findings.qaRationale: GS1's property describes the recycling process of packaging (COMPOSTABLE, REUSABLE), whereas our term identifies a fiber-based recycling technology for the textile product itself, so the subjects and concepts differ despite both naming 'recycling process'.

findings.rationale: Both refer to a standard applied to product testing, but the upstream term is broader (any certification) whereas ours specifically denotes a durability or robustness test standard.
findings.qaRationale: A certification standard (criteria for certifying conformity, free-text name) is a different concept from a durability/robustness test method standard, so they are not the same concept despite both containing the word 'standard'.

findings.rationale: Both refer to a standard used as a reference for testing or evaluation, but the upstream term is broader and not limited to durability or robustness tests.
findings.qaRationale: Our term names specifically the standard for a durability/robustness test, a narrower scope than upstream's general 'referenceStandard' for any conformity evaluation.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe the incentive offered for returning products, and they share the same name and intended use.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties capturing a return incentive and almost certainly the same concept, but the upstream core term has no definition to confirm full interchangeability, so the stricter grade is closeMatch rather than exact.

findings.rationale: Both refer to a monetary reduction, but the take‑back incentive is specifically tied to product returns, whereas discount applies generally to any order.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote a monetary reduction, but takeBackIncentive is a string description scoped to product-return programs while schema:discount is a general order-level discount, making ours the narrower concept.

findings.rationale: The eutex:sizeRange property specifies a product’s available size range as a string, whereas schema.org/size is a broader concept that can be expressed as a string or quantitative value; thus the eutex term is narrower.
findings.qaRationale: schema:size denotes a single product's standardized size, whereas eutex:sizeRange denotes the span of sizes offered, so they overlap on the size concept but are not in a clean subsumption relation.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies the actual size range of a product, whereas the candidate property refers to broader size categories or groups.
findings.qaRationale: schema:sizeGroup denotes a size segment/audience type (e.g. 'petite', 'big and tall'), whereas our property records the actual span of available sizes (XS-XXL), so they capture different sizing facets rather than a broader/narrower form of the same concept.

findings.rationale: The GS1 ‘Size’ property refers to a general linkage to size groupings and dimensions, whereas our ‘sizeRange’ provides a specific string of available sizes.
findings.qaRationale: Our sizeRange is a single xsd:string property listing the available size range, whereas gs1:size links a product to structured gs1:SizeDetails groupings, so ours expresses a narrower, simpler facet of the same size concept.

findings.rationale: Both terms refer to the mass of certified organic fibre content in a product, indicating identical concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is regulation-specific (certified organic fibre mass per EU 2018/848, textile domain) while the undefined upstream property shares the name but lacks a definition to confirm identical scope, so EXACT cannot be justified.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies units used for LCIA values, which is a specialized subset of the general measurement units defined by GS1.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties giving a measurement-unit string, but LCIA equivalence units like 'kg CO2-eq' are not UN/ECE Rec 20 codes, so ours is not a clean subset of gs1:unitCode—the concepts overlap without ours being a true narrowing.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies units for LCIA values, a specialized subset of the general measurement unit codes defined by Schema.org.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote a measurement unit, but schema:unitCode specifically expects a UN/CEFACT coded value whereas eutex:lciaUnit holds free-text composite impact-category units (e.g. 'kg CO2-eq') that are not UN/CEFACT codes, so ours is not a clean subset and the NARROW grade overstates the fit.

findings.rationale: eutex:endOfLifeHandling provides specific disposal instructions for a substance, whereas gs1:sustainabilityInfo refers to general sustainability and recycling information; the former is a narrower, more detailed concept.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a hazard-driven free-text disposal instruction scoped to a SubstanceOfConcern, whereas gs1:sustainabilityInfo is a link to general sustainability/recycling documentation about a product, so they are not the same concept despite tangential end-of-life overlap.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe production efficiency metrics, but yield measures final product quantity while waste percentage quantifies material discarded; they are related yet distinct.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:yield is a quantity of usable product after preparation while our term is a percentage of material wasted in production; these are inverse-related but distinct concepts with different ranges, so reject.

findings.rationale: eutex:TestStandard denotes a specific type of textile durability test standard, while UNTP Standard is a generic concept for any standard; the former is a narrower subclass.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes; eutex:TestStandard is a domain-specific subtype (textile durability/robustness test standards) of the generic untp:Standard concept, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Both properties specify the method used for a measurement, but our property is specific to spirality tests whereas the candidate is a general measurement method.
findings.qaRationale: Our property is a domain-specific specialization (spirality test method reference) of the general schema:measurementMethod that names the method used for a measurement, so it is narrower rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to a measure of product recyclability, but our term specifies the technical assessment concept while the candidate is a generic score value.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is the technical-dimension recyclability assessment concept, whereas the candidate is an undefined aggregate recyclability score value, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The Chain of Custody Method is a specific technique employed within a broader RecyclabilityAssessment.
findings.qaRationale: Chain-of-custody method (mass-balance/segregation tracking to substantiate recycled-content claims) is a provenance/traceability concept, not a sub-type of end-of-life recyclability assessment, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Weaving Facility refers specifically to a location where yarn is woven or knitted, which is a subset of the broader Manufacturing Plant concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are product-to-facility properties and a weaving/knitting facility is a specific type of manufacturing plant, so our term is a narrower specialization of gs1:manufacturingPlant.

findings.rationale: Textile Footwear is a specific type of footwear, making it narrower than the general Footwear class.
findings.qaRationale: Both are footwear classes, but our term restricts to textile-containing footwear, a proper subset of gs1:Footwear, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Textile Footwear is a specific type of Wearable Product, making it a narrower subclass.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes and textile footwear is a strict subset of products worn on the body, so our term is narrower than gs1:WearableProduct.

findings.rationale: Textile Footwear is a specific category of footwear, which falls under the broader category of Clothing.
findings.qaRationale: GS1 models Footwear as a sibling of Clothing (not a subclass), and our term explicitly bridges to gs1:Footwear, so mapping TextileFootwear under gs1:Clothing is a wrong-concept match rather than a genuine narrowing.

findings.rationale: Both properties record a country code, but the upstream term applies to any product while ours is specific to textile fibers.
findings.qaRationale: Fiber origin records where the raw textile material was grown/produced, which is a different referent than the finished product's country of origin (determined by substantial transformation), so ours is not a narrower form of the upstream concept but a distinct one.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a country code for fiber origin, whereas the candidate describes a general geographic area of origin; the upstream term is broader.
findings.qaRationale: Both are origin properties, but ours pins the fiber component's source as an ISO country code while the GS1 term is a free-text statement about the whole item's origin or processing, so they overlap without a clean subset relationship.

findings.rationale: Both properties record a country code, but the schema.org property applies to all products and creative works, whereas ours is specific to textile fibers.
findings.qaRationale: Both record a product's country of origin, but ours is a textile-specific fiber-origin attribute (domain gs1:TextileMaterialDetails, ISO 3166-1 code) while schema.org's is a generic product/creative-work property, so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: Both terms represent the same concept of product durability metrics, as indicated by identical labels and matching class type.
findings.qaRationale: The candidate IRI is identical to our own term (eutex: expands to https://ref.openepcis.io/extensions/eu/textile/), so this is a self-reference, not a genuine cross-vocabulary upstream alignment to grade.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric score, but PEF Single Score is specific to environmental footprint whereas ratingValue is a generic content rating.
findings.qaRationale: A PEFCR-defined environmental footprint impact score in mPt is a specific scientific measurement, not a generic content rating, so the only commonality is being numeric — a name/shape similarity, not a conceptual match.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies third‑party certification for recycled content claims, whereas hasCertification is a general certification property applicable to any product or entity.
findings.qaRationale: Our property is a specialized third-party certification of recycled-content claims on a RecycledContentDeclaration, a strict specialization of schema:hasCertification's general product/organization certification, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Our property is a specific third‑party certification for recycled content claims, whereas the GS1 property is a general certification information field.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties sharing the gs1:CertificationDetails range, but ours is gs1:certification scoped specifically to third-party verification of recycled-content claims, making it a narrower specialization.

findings.rationale: The ESPR Article 7(5) classification is a specific subset of the generic Substance concept defined by schema.org.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a classification scheme enumerating 4 regulatory types of substances of concern, not a substance (matter) itself, so it is not a subclass of schema:Substance but a categorically different kind of entity.

findings.rationale: chemicalPurpose specifies the purpose of a chemical substance, whereas potentialUse is a broader property for any BioChemEntity.
findings.qaRationale: schema:potentialUse denotes a BioChemEntity's intended use by humans, a distinct concept from the functional role a hazardous substance plays in a textile (dye, finish), so they are not the same property despite surface overlap.

findings.rationale: EnvironmentalFootprint covers overall environmental impact, including carbon footprint and benchmark scores, whereas EmissionsPerformance focuses specifically on emissions metrics.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern climate/emissions impact and overlap on carbon footprint, but ours is a footprint declaration framing while EmissionsPerformance (undefined) reads as an emissions assessment/rating, so they overlap without clean subsumption.

findings.rationale: Both represent a numeric value qualified by a unit, but the upstream term is generic measurement while ours is specific to LCIA impact category.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:value is a generic numeric-value-qualified-by-unit primitive that fully subsumes our LCIA-category-specific value, so ours is a narrower specialization rather than an interchangeable or merely-overlapping concept.

findings.rationale: Both represent a numeric value used in quantitative contexts, but schema.org's 'value' is generic while our term specifically denotes an LCIA impact category value.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org's value is a generic structured-value/property-value slot, not a life-cycle impact assessment metric, so this is name-similarity not a concept match; reject per class-to-class/property-to-property meaning test.

findings.rationale: Our property refers to the overall take‑back program, while the upstream property specifies a URL that points to such a program.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a property whose range is a TakeBackProgram object carrying full program information, whereas the upstream property carries only a URL string pointing to a program, so they denote different concepts (a program record vs. a link to one).

findings.rationale: colourChangeRating is a specific numeric sub‑rating for visual inspection, whereas ratingValue is a generic content rating; the former is narrower than the latter.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a property whose domain is a textile visual-inspection result holding an ISO 15487 colour-change sub-rating, a specific physical-property measurement, whereas schema:ratingValue is a generic property of a Rating about content/products and the two are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the percentage of organic material by weight in a product, matching definition and range.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is restricted to certified organic fibre content by mass under EU 2018/848, whereas the GS1 property is a broader, general organic-materials-by-weight claim for any product, so ours is the narrower concept rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both terms refer to the percentage of organic content in a product, though the upstream definition is missing.
findings.qaRationale: Identical name and property type suggest the same concept family, but the upstream term carries no definition while ours is narrowly scoped to certified organic fibre by mass under EU 2018/848, so identity cannot be confirmed and only an overlapping match is defensible.

findings.rationale: Organic Content Percentage is a specific instance of Ingredient Content Percentage, thus it is narrower.
findings.qaRationale: GS1's term measures one named ingredient's share of total ingredients (paired with ingredientName), whereas ours is a certification-status mass fraction of organic fibre and is not an ingredient-composition value, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The upstream class describes general textile material composition, while our class focuses specifically on microplastic shedding information.
findings.qaRationale: Microplastic shedding potential is an environmental-behavior characteristic, not a subtype of textile material composition, so the upstream composition class does not subsume our concept.

findings.rationale: Both express a weight‑based percentage of material, but the upstream term covers any textile material while ours specifically refers to secondary (recycled) material.
findings.qaRationale: Both are weight-based material percentages, but ours is restricted to secondary/recycled material while the GS1 term covers any textile material component, making ours a narrower specialization.

findings.rationale: The candidate property describes overall textile material composition with percentages, while the OUR term specifies only the secondary (recycled) fraction by weight.
findings.qaRationale: GS1 textileMaterialContent describes fiber composition by type (e.g. cotton/polyester percentages), whereas our property captures the recycled-vs-virgin provenance fraction, a distinct axis that is not a sub-concept of material composition.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide free‑text instructions for the consumer, though one focuses on care and the other on general usage.
findings.qaRationale: Care/maintenance instructions (washing, drying, ironing) are a distinct concept from general product usage instructions, and this is a supplementary free-text field within a CareInstruction structure rather than the same property.

findings.rationale: The upstream property specifies storage instructions, a narrower subset of the general care instructions described by our term.
findings.qaRationale: Our free-text textile care-maintenance catch-all and GS1's product storage instructions are distinct concepts, and storage is not a genuine subset of care instructions, so there is no valid graded match.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a visual inspection test method, which is a specific type of measurement technique covered by schema.org’s generic measurementMethod.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties naming the method used; our visual-inspection test-method reference is a domain-specific specialization of schema.org's generic measurementMethod, hence narrower.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a structured declaration of recycled content for a product, matching in scope and intended use.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties about recycled content, but ours ranges over a structured RecycledContentDeclaration object while the upstream's undefined 'recycledContent' likely carries a simpler value, so they overlap without being demonstrably interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Recycled Content Declaration is a specific declaration of recycled material within the overall textile composition, making it narrower than the general Textile Material Content.
findings.qaRationale: GS1 textileMaterialContent describes fiber composition by percentage (what the textile is made of), whereas our term is a recycled-origin/provenance sustainability declaration answering a different question, so it is neither a narrower form nor an interchangeable match.

findings.rationale: Both properties are numeric ratings, but the spirality score is a specific, domain‑restricted measure whereas ratingValue is a general content rating.
findings.qaRationale: A domain-restricted integer sub-score (0-3) for a textile spirality test is not the same concept as schema.org's generic content ratingValue, and a discipline-specific scoring property maps to none of it.

findings.rationale: A Take‑Back Program encompasses the act of returning, so it is a broader concept than ReturnAction.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is an organized end-of-life collection scheme (a program/information class), whereas schema:ReturnAction is a single act/event of returning ownership; a program is not a broader subsumer of an action but a categorically different concept, so they do not align.

findings.rationale: Take‑Back Program is a specific type of return policy, whereas MerchantReturnEnumeration lists various general return policies.
findings.qaRationale: A circular-economy end-of-life take-back program is a different concept from schema.org's enumeration of merchant return-for-refund policy types, and a program-information class cannot be a narrower kind of an enumeration type.

findings.rationale: A take‑back program is a specific type of service, so it is narrower than the general Service class.
findings.qaRationale: schema:Service is a generic catch-all for any organizational service, not the concept of an end-of-life take-back/collection program, so this is loose category resemblance rather than a genuine narrower-concept match.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same name, domain (TakeBackProgram), and range (xsd:anyURI) indicating they represent the same concept.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream property shares the identical name and a take-back URL concept, but with no upstream definition or domain to confirm true interchangeability the stricter call is overlapping-not-identical.

findings.rationale: The GS1 sustainabilityInfo link covers all sustainability and recycling information, while the takeBackUrl specifically points to take‑back program details.
findings.qaRationale: Both are link-type properties and a take-back program URL is a specific subset of GS1's general sustainability-and-recycling information link, so OUR term is narrower.

findings.rationale: The candidate property covers any additional product classification, while the apparelSubcategory is a specific sub‑classification within that broader category.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classification properties on a product and apparelSubcategory is a specific kind of additional product classification, so ours is the narrower term, though the fit is approximate since GS1's property targets generic classification-detail objects rather than a controlled apparel taxonomy.

findings.rationale: schema.org category is a general item classification, whereas eutex:apparelSubcategory specifies a particular apparel subcategory.
findings.qaRationale: schema:category is a generic item-classification property whose value space subsumes apparel subcategories, so our EU-specific apparel subcategory property is a narrower specialization of it.

findings.rationale: Both properties are named safeUseInstructions and serve the same purpose of providing instructions for safe use.
findings.qaRationale: Both are safe-use-instruction properties, but ours is scoped to the SubstanceOfConcern domain while the upstream core term has no definition or stated domain, so identity cannot be confirmed and they only clearly overlap.

findings.rationale: The GS1 property covers general consumer usage instructions, while the eutex term focuses specifically on safe use instructions for a substance of concern.
findings.qaRationale: Both are product usage-instruction properties, but ours is scoped to safe-handling of a substance of concern (domain eutex:SubstanceOfConcern) while the GS1 property is general product-level consumer usage text, so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: Consumer Safety Information is a general safety description, whereas safeUseInstructions provides specific instructions for safe use of the substance.
findings.qaRationale: Safe-use instructions tied to a substance of concern are a specific subset of the broader product-level consumer safety information, so our term is narrower (the first pass picked BROAD but its own rationale describes ours as the more specific term).

findings.rationale: Safe Use Instructions provide specific usage guidance for a substance, whereas safetyConsideration describes general safety concerns; the former is a narrower, more detailed concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our term captures actionable safe-use instructions tied to a substance of concern in textiles, whereas schema.org's supplement-scoped property documents potential safety concerns/risks, a different concept with an incompatible domain.

findings.rationale: robustnessAssessment refers to a general assessment of textile robustness, whereas robustnessScore is a specific numeric score.
findings.qaRationale: Both are product-level properties about textile robustness, but ours yields a full RobustnessAssessment object that would encompass the single numeric robustnessScore the upstream property captures, making ours broader.

findings.rationale: robustnessAssessment is a specific assessment of textile robustness, whereas hasMeasurement is a generic measurement property.
findings.qaRationale: Our term ranges over a structured eutex:RobustnessAssessment object (a domain-specific composite assessment of durability), not a schema:QuantitativeValue measurement, so it is a distinct concept rather than a narrower kind of hasMeasurement.

findings.rationale: Both describe a numeric evaluation, but the visual inspection result is a specific type of rating defined by ISO 15487.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes representing a numeric-scale evaluation, and the ISO 15487 visual inspection result is a domain-specific specialization of the generic schema.org Rating.

findings.rationale: Maximum Concentration is a general weight‑percentage of any substance, while Juice Content Percent refers specifically to the juice portion of a product.
findings.qaRationale: Our term measures the weight-percentage of a substance of concern in a regulated textile product, whereas the GS1 term measures fruit juice content of a beverage; sharing a percentage unit is not a conceptual overlap, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same label and refer to an EU Declaration of Conformity document, indicating identical concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties naming the EU Declaration of Conformity and clearly target the same concept, but the upstream term provides no definition to confirm it is a document reference with interchangeable range/semantics, so the weaker CLOSE grade is warranted over EXACT.

findings.rationale: RecycledContentDeclaration is a detailed, structured declaration of recycled content, whereas RecycledContent is a more general class for recycled material information.
findings.qaRationale: Our eutex:RecycledContentDeclaration is a structured wrapper aggregating recycled-content attributes (secondary fraction, waste origin, source type, chain of custody) so it is broader than the upstream oec:RecycledContent which appears to denote the recycled-content value/fact itself, not a NARROW specialization of it.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a composite recyclability score for a product, with identical domain and range.
findings.qaRationale: Both are recyclability-score properties so the concept clearly overlaps, but the upstream term has no definition, domain, or range, so identical/interchangeable semantics (e.g. our 0-10 composite scale) cannot be confirmed and EXACT is unwarranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a composite recyclability score for a product, with identical semantics and domain/range.
findings.qaRationale: Both are recyclability-score properties from the same project, but the upstream core term has no definition or stated range while ours is a specific 0-10 composite scoped to eutex:RecyclabilityAssessment, so identity cannot be confirmed and the weaker overlapping grade is safer.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric rating value, but schema.org’s ratingValue is generic while recyclabilityScore is specifically for recyclability assessment.
findings.qaRationale: Both are numeric rating properties, but schema.org's ratingValue is a generic rating tied to schema:Rating while eutex:recyclabilityScore is a domain-specific composite recyclability metric on RecyclabilityAssessment, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties link a product to its recyclability assessment, representing the same relationship.
findings.qaRationale: Our textile-specific property (range eutex:RecyclabilityAssessment, scoped to textile products) is a Layer-4 specialization of the generic cross-cutting core property, so ours is narrower rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe a range of acceptable or relevant values, though the medical context of normalRange differs from the confidential concentration range for substances.
findings.qaRationale: normalRange is a medical property denoting acceptable physiological values for a typical patient, which shares only the generic notion of 'range' with a confidential substance concentration range and is not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The cut and sew facility is a specific location where assembly occurs, whereas countryOfAssembly refers to the broader geographic region.
findings.qaRationale: Ours identifies a specific cut-and-sew facility (range FacilityInformation), whereas countryOfAssembly records a country-level place of assembly; the differing value types and textile-specific operation make them distinct concepts rather than a clean narrower/broader pair.

findings.rationale: The upstream property indicates the country where a product is assembled, whereas ours specifies the particular facility where garment cutting and sewing occur.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern where the product is assembled, but ours names a specific cut-and-sew facility (range FacilityInformation) while the upstream property yields a country, so they overlap in concept without a clean subset relation.

findings.rationale: The cut and sew facility is a specific type of manufacturing plant, so it is narrower than the general Has Manufacturing Plant property.
findings.qaRationale: Both are product-to-facility properties and a cut-and-sew (garment assembly) site is a specific kind of manufacturing plant, so our term is a narrower specialization of gs1:manufacturingPlant.

findings.rationale: The upstream property denotes a general location of an organization, while ours specifies the particular facility where garment cutting and sewing occur.
findings.qaRationale: Our cut-and-sew facility property is a specialized subtype of the upstream general organization-location property, designating one specific production-step location.

findings.rationale: Both classes represent the same concept of a recyclability assessment, and share identical labels.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a textile-specific recyclability assessment defined by the EU Preparatory Study 3rd Milestone scoring (elastane thresholds, sorting factors), making it a specialization of the generic core RecyclabilityAssessment rather than interchangeable with it.

findings.rationale: RecyclabilityAssessment is a specific assessment, whereas CircularityPerformance is a broader concept covering overall circularity.
findings.qaRationale: Recyclability is one dimension of circularity, so our recyclability-specific scoring class falls under the broader CircularityPerformance class, though the upstream term's missing definition leaves some uncertainty.

findings.rationale: RecyclabilityAssessment is a specific type of numeric rating, whereas Rating is a generic evaluation on any numeric scale.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a domain-specific recyclability evaluation yielding a 0-10 score, which overlaps schema.org Rating's numeric-evaluation concept but is not a clean subtype since the assessment also encodes methodology, thresholds, and criteria that Rating (a bare value-on-a-scale) does not model.

findings.rationale: Robustness Assessment is a specific numeric rating for cleaning durability, whereas Rating denotes any generic numeric evaluation.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes and our composite 0-10 robustness score is a domain-specific instance of a numeric evaluation on a scale, making it a narrower specialization of schema.org Rating.

findings.rationale: RobustnessAssessment is a specific durability test for textiles, whereas DurabilityInfo represents general durability information.
findings.qaRationale: Both are textile durability classes and robustness is a component of durability, but the upstream term has no definition and frames a general info container versus our specific scored assessment, so overlap is established while strict subsumption cannot be confirmed.

findings.rationale: RobustnessAssessment is a specific test result, while Evidence refers to any proof that a requirement is met.
findings.qaRationale: A composite test-based robustness score is a measurement/assessment result, not a subtype of CCCEV Evidence (a proof artifact that a requirement is met), so the class-level concepts do not align.

findings.rationale: Recycled Content Source specifies the origin of recycled material, whereas recycledContent refers to the recycled material itself.
findings.qaRationale: recycledContentSource names the origin/feedstock of recycled material while oec:recycledContent denotes the recycled content itself, so ours is a narrower facet of that broader concept.

findings.rationale: Recycled Content Source specifies the origin of recycled material, whereas Sustainability and recycling is a broader link to general sustainability information.
findings.qaRationale: Upstream sustainabilityInfo is a generic link/reference to sustainability-and-recycling documentation, whereas our term is a specific string datum naming the origin of recycled material; differing concept and value type make this not the same concept rather than a true narrowing.

findings.rationale: Location in Product specifies a specific part of the product where a substance is found, whereas Contained in Place refers to a larger physical location that contains sub‑locations.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a free-text descriptor of where a chemical substance sits within a product's components, while gs1:containedInPlace expresses a physical/geographic location-within-location containment hierarchy referencing Place objects, so they are unrelated concepts despite superficial 'location' naming.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the method used for a measurement or test, but our term is specific to dimensional change tests whereas schema.org’s measurementMethod is a general subproperty for any measurement technique.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties naming the method behind a measurement/test, and ours is a domain-restricted specialization (dimensional-change test method) of schema.org's general measurementMethod, so ours is narrower rather than merely overlapping.

findings.rationale: Both refer to concerning substances within a product, but our term is specific to textile products per ESPR Article 7(5), whereas the GS1 property covers any ingredient of concern in a product.
findings.qaRationale: Our term names a structured SubstanceOfConcern set per ESPR Art.7(5), while the GS1 ingredientOfConcern is a single-ingredient claim, so ours is the broader/aggregating property rather than a subset of it.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the set of substances of concern present in a textile product, sharing identical domain and range.
findings.qaRationale: Both are 'substances of concern' properties, but ours is scoped to the textile product per ESPR Art 7(5) with a textile-specific range while the oec: core term is the cross-cutting general version, making ours a specialization rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The manufacturing‑phase footprint is a specific subset of the overall total carbon footprint.
findings.qaRationale: Both are carbon-footprint properties but scoped differently — manufacturing-phase is a partitive component of a lifecycle total, not a SKOS narrower-kind-of it, so they overlap without one subsuming the other and are not interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties express CO₂ emissions, but the schema.org term uses a different unit (g/km) and context; they overlap conceptually.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org emissionsCO2 is a vehicle's CO2 emission rate in g/km, a fundamentally different concept and dimension from a product's cradle-to-gate manufacturing carbon footprint in kg CO2-eq per unit.

findings.rationale: The property represents a specific quantitative value (kg CO₂‑eq) for manufacturing, whereas schema.org/value is a generic container for any property value.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific carbon-footprint manufacturing measurement property whereas schema.org/value is a generic value-container property for QuantitativeValue/PropertyValue nodes, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Textile Apparel is a specific type of Wearable Product, making it a narrower subclass.
findings.qaRationale: Textile Apparel (clothing) is a proper subset of gs1:WearableProduct, which also covers non-apparel worn items like footwear, eyewear, and jewelry, so our class-to-class match is genuinely narrower.

findings.rationale: Our class represents a specific type of clothing (textile apparel), which is a narrower subclass of the general Clothing class.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes; textile apparel is a specific subset of clothing-worn-on-the-body (excluding non-textile clothing), so our class is properly narrower than gs1:Clothing.

findings.rationale: Fabric Type is a specific construction category of textile material, making it narrower than the general Textile Material class.
findings.qaRationale: Our FabricType is about fabric construction/structure (woven, knit) driving scoring thresholds, whereas gs1:TextileMaterialDetails concerns fiber composition of materials, a different aspect of textiles rather than a broader genus of ours.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same name, domain (gs1:Product), and describe product weight excluding trims.
findings.qaRationale: Identical names and matching property type/domain suggest the same concept, but the upstream term provides no definition, so EXACT interchangeability cannot be confirmed and the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies product weight excluding trims, a more specific subset of the general weight concept in schema.org.
findings.qaRationale: Both are weight properties and our term is a specialized weight metric (excluding trims) that is a more specific subset of schema.org's generic product weight.

findings.rationale: The repairGuideUrl property specifies a URL for a DIY repair guide, which is a specific type of internet resource covered by the broader referencedFileURL property.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:referencedFileURL is a generic any-resource file-reference catch-all that carries none of the repair-guide/repairability semantics that are the entire point of our term, so it is not the same concept rather than a true conceptual parent.

findings.rationale: The upstream property refers to a general link to instructions, while our term specifies a repair/care guide URL, making it a narrower specialization.
findings.qaRationale: GS1 instructions is a general instructions link (assembly, usage, etc.) and our repair/care guide URL is a specific subtype of that link, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Durability Class is a specific numeric rating for textiles, while Rating is a generic evaluation scale.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes about evaluation, but a textile durability class is a categorical classification rather than a clean specialization of schema:Rating's numeric-scale evaluation, so they overlap without a strict narrower-than relationship.

findings.rationale: Both terms represent the same discrete performance class for manufacturing carbon footprint relative to a benchmark.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term carries no definition, so identical naming suggests the same discrete carbon-footprint banding concept but cannot confirm interchangeability with our PEFCR-benchmarked integer-band specification, warranting a downgrade from EXACT to CLOSE.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a discrete class of environmental footprint, matching in scope and intended use.
findings.qaRationale: Carbon (climate-change) footprint is one specific impact category within the broader environmental footprint, so our class band is narrower than the upstream environmentalFootprintClass, not interchangeable with it.

findings.rationale: The OUR property includes both season and year (e.g., FW2024), whereas the candidate only specifies the calendar year, making OUR a more specific (narrower) concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are seasonal-availability properties on a product, but ours encodes season+year as a combined string (FW2024) while the GS1 term captures only the calendar year, so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: Our property encodes a season code with year (e.g., FW2024), whereas the GS1 property only specifies the season name, making it a more general concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both describe a product's seasonal classification, but ours is a composite collection code bundling season plus year (FW2024) rather than a clean subtype of GS1's bare season name (Winter), so the value spaces overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: The OUR property captures both season and year (e.g., FW2024), whereas the candidate only specifies a season code, making OUR more specific.
findings.qaRationale: Both encode a product season but ours bundles season-with-year as a fashion collection identifier (FW2024) while the GS1 term is a plain availability-season code (SPRING/WINTER), so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: The OUR property focuses specifically on textile care and maintenance, whereas the candidate covers general consumer usage instructions for any product.
findings.qaRationale: Care/maintenance instructions and consumer usage instructions overlap as label-borne guidance but are distinct concepts (maintenance vs. how-to-use), and the structured eutex:CareInstruction range versus free-text usage instructions prevents a clean subset relation.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the presence of the EU Ecolabel certification for a product.
findings.qaRationale: Identical property name and plausibly the same EU Ecolabel concept, but the upstream term has no definition or range so interchangeability cannot be confirmed; downgrade from EXACT to the weaker grade.

findings.rationale: The candidate property represents a general accreditation code, while our term specifies the particular EU Ecolabel certification.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a boolean flag asserting presence of one specific certification, while the GS1 property is an accreditation/label code value and explicitly excludes regulatory claims, so they are different concepts not a genus-species match.

findings.rationale: Both terms are named robustnessScore and refer to a composite score for assessing robustness, indicating they represent the same concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a textile-specific composite (0-10, summing spirality/dimensional-change/visual-inspection per JRC) while the undefined upstream robustnessScore is presumably a generic DPP robustness measure, so ours is narrower rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric rating, but the upstream term is generic for any content while ours is a specific composite robustness score.
findings.qaRationale: schema:ratingValue expresses a subjective rating of content/reviews, whereas our term is an objective composite durability test measurement, so the numeric overlap is superficial and the concepts differ.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a score indicating recyclability, though the upstream term is more generic while ours specifies technical recyclability based on applicable technology.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific technical-recyclability component sub-score (domain TechnicalRecyclability), which is a narrower facet of the upstream's generic overall recyclabilityScore.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric score for recyclability, and the upstream term is intended to capture the same concept of a recyclability metric.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a technology-specific component subscore (domain TechnicalRecyclability), narrower than the upstream generic overall recyclabilityScore, so it is a subconcept rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties hold a numeric rating value, but our property is specifically a technical recyclability score while the schema.org property is a generic rating for any content.
findings.qaRationale: Our property is a domain-specific technical recyclability sub-score on eutex:TechnicalRecyclability, not the same concept as schema.org's generic content rating, so it should anchor to a domain measure rather than ratingValue.

findings.rationale: The candidate property describes a general manufacturing plant, whereas our property refers specifically to the facility where finishing treatments were applied.
findings.qaRationale: A finishing facility is a specialized type of manufacturing plant (one performing the finishing sub-process), so our property is a narrower specialization of the broader gs1:manufacturingPlant relation, though role ambiguity about which facility is referenced keeps confidence moderate.

findings.rationale: The spinning facility is a specific type of manufacturing plant, making it a narrower concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties linking a product to a production facility, and a spinning facility is a specific kind of manufacturing plant, so our term is a genuine narrowing of gs1:manufacturingPlant.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same concept of a discrete performance class for environmental footprint, as indicated by identical labels and matching domain/range.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term shares the identical label and is a property, but its missing definition makes its exact semantics (integer band, PEF single score, category-benchmark relativity) unverifiable, so interchangeability cannot be confirmed and only an approximate overlap can be asserted.

findings.rationale: Both properties classify a product’s environmental or carbon footprint into discrete bands, though the DPP term lacks explicit definition and scope.
findings.qaRationale: Carbon footprint is a single climate-change impact category whereas our term classifies the multi-criteria PEF single score, so the two properties band fundamentally different underlying metrics and are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties express a numeric rating, but the candidate is generic for content while ours specifically refers to textile pilling resistance.
findings.qaRationale: schema:ratingValue is a generic review/rating value for content (stars, scores on a Rating object), whereas our term is a specific ISO 12945 textile durability measurement, so they share only the word "rating" and not the underlying concept.

findings.rationale: The candidate class is a generic category code, while our class specifies LCIA category codes per PEF methodology.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes representing category codes; ours is a domain-specific specialization restricted to PEF LCIA impact-assessment categories, making it strictly narrower than schema:CategoryCode's generic any-categorization-system code.

findings.rationale: The candidate class represents a set of category codes, whereas our class denotes an individual LCIA category code.
findings.qaRationale: Our term denotes an individual LCIA category code (matching schema:CategoryCode), whereas CategoryCodeSet is a container/collection of codes; a member is not a narrower subtype of its set, so this is a different concept.

findings.rationale: LCIA Category Code is a specific code list for PEF categories, whereas TypeCode is a generic code list.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:TypeCode is a generic abstract base for any code list and carries no environmental/PEF semantics, so anchoring an LCIA impact-category code list to it is structural name-matching rather than a genuine concept correspondence.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric rating value, but our score is specifically an integer 0‑4 for visual inspection, whereas ratingValue can be any numeric value.
findings.qaRationale: schema:ratingValue is the rating-of-content value used in Review/Rating structures, whereas ours is a regulatory visual-inspection scoring component (0-4 points) on an inspection result; sharing a numeric range is name/shape similarity, not the same concept.

findings.rationale: SortingFactors are a specific set of criteria used within a broader RecyclabilityAssessment.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a collection of discrete scoring criteria (a part/input of recyclability scoring), not a kind of assessment, and the upstream class has no definition to justify a hierarchical match, so a positive graded relation isn't warranted.

findings.rationale: Robustness Test Fabric Type is a specific measured property, whereas measuredProperty is a generic concept for any observed measurement.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a configuration/classification property whose value is a FabricType selecting which scoring table applies, not the measuredProperty of a schema.org Observation, so they are different concepts.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the European Community (EINECS/ELINCS) identifier for a substance.
findings.qaRationale: Both name the same EINECS/ELINCS European Community number identifier property; the textile term simply scopes the core oec:ecNumber to SubstanceOfConcern without changing the identifier's meaning, so they are interchangeable.

findings.rationale: EC Number is a specific chemical identifier, whereas catalogNumber is a generic product reference number.
findings.qaRationale: EC Number is a regulated chemical substance identifier (EINECS/ELINCS) on a SubstanceOfConcern, whereas schema.org catalogNumber is a generic product catalog reference for a release/product, so they denote unrelated concepts rather than a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: The EC Number is a specific type of identifier, whereas schema.org/identifier represents any kind of identifier.
findings.qaRationale: eutex:ecNumber is a specific kind of textual identifier (EINECS/ELINCS) and schema.org/identifier is the generic identifier supertype, so ours is strictly narrower.

findings.rationale: The upstream property is a general certification indicator, while the OUR property specifies fiber‑specific certifications.
findings.qaRationale: Our fiber-specific certification property is a specialization of schema.org's general product/organization certification property, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: The upstream property is a general certification indicator for products, whereas the OUR property applies only to fiber‑specific certifications.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a certification relation, but ours restricts the domain to textile fiber material and scopes to fiber-specific certifications, making it a specialization of the upstream general certification property.

findings.rationale: The OUR class specifies a particular type of substance (concerned substances in textiles), whereas schema.org Substance is a general class of any matter.
findings.qaRationale: Every substance of concern is extensionally a kind of substance, but our class is defined by its ESPR Article 7(5) regulatory-hazard classification rather than by being generic matter, so the concepts overlap without a clean genus-species subsumption and NARROW overstates the alignment.

findings.rationale: The term denotes a specific type of chemical substance that is a concern in textiles, which is a narrower subset of the general ChemicalSubstance class.
findings.qaRationale: OUR term is a regulatory hazard-classification concept (substances flagged as concerning per ESPR Article 7(5) Types A-D), whereas schema:ChemicalSubstance models matter of constant composition; the defining axis is regulatory concern not chemical identity, so it is not a true subclass.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric rating value, but the upstream property is generic for content ratings whereas ours is specific to color fastness.
findings.qaRationale: schema:ratingValue is the numeric value of a schema:Rating (reviews/content ratings); our term is a specific ISO 105 physical color-fastness measurement, so the shared word 'rating' is name-similarity, not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric rating value, but the OUR term is limited to an integer 1‑5 per ISO 15487, whereas schema.org/ratingValue accepts any numeric rating.
findings.qaRationale: schema:ratingValue is the value of a subjective Rating about content (reviews/quality assessments), whereas our term is an objective standardized ISO 15487 fabric-surface test grade, so the shared word 'rating' reflects name similarity rather than the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same concept of providing information about available repair services for a product.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term has no definition to confirm interchangeability, and our property specifically ranges over the providing gs1:Organization while the name-matched upstream term's scope is unverifiable, so a same-concept overlap (CLOSE) is the strongest defensible grade rather than EXACT.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to general service or maintenance instructions, whereas the OUR term specifically denotes repair services provided by an organization.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties about post-sale service, but ours covers only repair services while gs1:serviceInfo links to general service or maintenance instructions, making ours narrower in scope.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric rating value, though the schema.org property is generic for content while ours is specific to seam appearance.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a domain-specific ISO 15487 seam-appearance sub-rating constrained to integers 1-5 on a VisualInspectionResult, whereas schema:ratingValue is a generic content-rating value with no shared semantics beyond both being numeric, so it is not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both express a percentage of a component, but the GS1 term refers to ingredient content relative to total ingredients, whereas ours specifies substance concentration by weight in the product.
findings.qaRationale: GS1's term measures a deliberate ingredient as a fraction of total ingredients for composition labeling, whereas ours measures a (often trace, possibly contaminant) substance-of-concern concentration by weight of the whole product for regulatory hazard reporting — different denominators, different domains, and different intent.

findings.rationale: Both properties express a percentage of a component in the product, but our term covers any substance concentration while the GS1 property is specific to fruit juice content.
findings.qaRationale: Our generic substance-of-concern concentration (any hazardous substance, w/w%) and GS1's juiceContentPercent (fruit juice content of a beverage) are unrelated concepts despite both being percentages, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The eutex:substanceConcentration property specifies a numeric percentage concentration, whereas schema.org/value is a generic value for any quantitative or textual data; thus our term is narrower.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/value is a generic structural value-carrier slot for QuantitativeValue/PropertyValue nodes, not the semantic concept of a substance's percent-by-weight concentration, so it is not the same concept and the apparent narrowing is only a name/type coincidence.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric value indicating a standard or score, but the GS1 term refers to a certification’s standard value while ours is a specific test component score.
findings.qaRationale: A textile dimensional-change test component score (0-3) is not the same concept as a certification's generic standard value; shared numeric form is not conceptual overlap.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to a category code, but the upstream property is generic while ours specifically denotes an LCIA impact category code.
findings.qaRationale: schema:hasCategoryCode links a CategoryCodeSet to the codes it contains (set-membership), whereas ours assigns an identifying code to a single LCIA impact category, so the relationship semantics differ rather than ours being a subset of theirs.

findings.rationale: The upstream property ‘recycledContent’ is a general reference to recycled material, whereas our property specifies the type of recycling pathway.
findings.qaRationale: Upstream 'recycledContent' denotes the recycled material content (typically its amount/proportion), whereas our property captures the recycling pathway type (e.g. pre- vs post-consumer); these are distinct facets, not a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: The GS1 property refers to general sustainability and recycling information, whereas our property specifies a particular type of recycling pathway.
findings.qaRationale: GS1 sustainabilityInfo is a hyperlink to general sustainability/recycling documentation, whereas our term is a typed enumeration classifying a specific recycling pathway, so they are different concepts rather than a broad/narrow pair.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies the percentage of synthetic fibers, a specific subset of textile material composition, whereas the upstream term covers any textile material percentage.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:textileMaterialPercentage is a per-material net-weight share paired with a single textileMaterialDescription, whereas ours is an aggregate synthetic-fiber percentage framed for microplastic shedding, so the concepts overlap but ours is not a clean subset of the upstream's per-material semantics.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide a reference string that supplies additional information about an associated value, though the specific context differs.
findings.qaRationale: schema:valueReference qualifies a measured value with a secondary value (e.g. reference temperature), whereas pefcrReference identifies which PEFCR methodology document/version applies, so they share only a name and not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to a general regulation reference, whereas our term specifies a particular PEFCR version string.
findings.qaRationale: A PEFCR is a methodological category-rules document referenced as a version string, not a regulation, so it is not the same concept as a reference to the governing regulation.

findings.rationale: RecycledSourceType specifies a particular recycling pathway, which is a more specific subclass of the broader RecycledContent concept.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a class enumerating recycling-pathway types (an attribute domain), whereas RecycledContent denotes the recycled-material content concept itself, so they are different concepts rather than a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: Both properties indicate whether a product contains non‑textile parts of animal origin.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties about non-textile parts of animal origin, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm identical boolean semantics and scope, so interchangeability cannot be asserted.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to an environmental footprint declaration for a product, but the candidate lacks a definition and range specification.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a footprint declaration object, whereas the candidate's 'Class' suffix and absent definition indicate a graded classification/category value rather than the same declaration concept, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The upstream property refers only to total carbon footprint, while our property covers the entire environmental footprint.
findings.qaRationale: Environmental footprint conceptually subsumes carbon footprint as one of several impact indicators, so ours is broader, though the structured-declaration range versus a single total-value differs enough to keep confidence modest.

findings.rationale: Our property covers overall environmental footprint, while the candidate refers specifically to carbon footprint, a narrower subset.
findings.qaRationale: Our property is a structured environmental-footprint declaration object, whereas carbonFootprintClass denotes a categorical carbon-rating band (a different kind of value, undefined upstream), so they are not the same concept rather than merely broader/narrower.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to a link for purchasing supplies or accessories in general, whereas our term specifically targets spare parts.
findings.qaRationale: Both are product-scoped purchase-link properties, but spare parts (replacement components) are not a strict subset of 'supplies or accessories' (consumables/optional add-ons), so the relation is overlapping rather than a clean narrowing.

findings.rationale: The schema.org url property is a general URL for an item, while our property specifically denotes the URL to purchase spare parts.
findings.qaRationale: schema:url denotes the canonical URL of the item itself, whereas sparePartsUrl points to a separate spare-parts purchasing resource, so it is a different concept rather than a specialization.

findings.rationale: The schema.org serviceUrl refers to a general website for accessing a service, whereas our property specifically denotes the URL used to purchase spare parts.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org serviceUrl is the website to reach a ServiceChannel/contact point for a service, not a commerce link to purchase spare parts, so they denote different concepts rather than a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a URL for spare parts, which is a narrower use of the generic website URL defined by the candidate.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:referencedFileURL is a generic document/resource reference URL, not a commerce link concept, so treating our purchase-spare-parts URL as merely a narrower instance overstates a name-level URL resemblance rather than a true shared meaning.

findings.rationale: The candidate class is a general recyclability assessment, while our term specifies technical recyclability based on technology.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific technical-recyclability assessment scoped to applicable recycling technology, making it a narrower kind of the upstream general RecyclabilityAssessment class.

findings.rationale: CircularityPerformance is a broader concept covering overall circularity metrics, while TechnicalRecyclability specifically assesses recyclability based on technology.
findings.qaRationale: Technical recyclability overlaps with circularity performance as one facet of it, but an assessment-of-recyclability class is not a clean hierarchical subtype of an undefined performance class, so the relation is overlapping rather than strict subsumption.

findings.rationale: The GS1 property gives a generic percentage for any textile material, whereas our term specifies the percentage of elastane/spandex only.
findings.qaRationale: Both are decimal material-percentage properties, but ours fixes the material to elastane (a specific case of GS1's generic per-material percentage), making ours a genuine specialization.

findings.rationale: The candidate property describes any textile material composition with percentages, while our term specifies only the elastane/spandex content.
findings.qaRationale: Both are textile-composition properties and ours concerns one material's percentage, but GS1's term is a composite material-plus-percentage description rather than a bare per-material decimal, so they overlap without a clean genus-species subsumption.
